coredata.dc:title: Theoretical insights into the activity of the N doped graphene quantum dots for oxygen reduction reaction as a function of the shape and edge terminations

Isac, Dragoș Lucian
Soriga, Ștefan Gabriel
Man, Isabela-Costinela
coredata.dc:description: We have investigated the possible activity of the N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQD) toward the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) using density functional theory (DFT) method. The effect of the shape (triangular (N-GQDT), rhombohedral (N-GQDR) and hexagonal (N-GQDH)), connected both with the zigzag (zz) and armchair (ac) edge terminations of the GQD and with the location of the N atom across the sheets are investigated. The activity of part of the studied N-GQDTac, GQDRac, N-GQDHac and N-GQDHzz structures improves significantly compared to the similar pristine structures. On the other hand, no matter on the location of the N in the N-GQDTzz and in the N-GQDRzz, their activity gets worsen, when compared with the activity of the pristine structures or with the activity of the other four investigated structures. N-GQDTac and N-GQDRac are the structures with the most possible locations of N, that generates active sites. CN bond breaking takes place in the triangular shapes (N-GQDTzz and N-GQDTac) when oxygen adsorption takes place. These bonds are less likely to regenerate and new reaction sites are created. The new reaction site created on the N-GQDTzz represents a possible active site compared to the site generated on the N-GQDTac, that is highly inactive.

The sites under investigation are emphasized by the blue semitransparent circle (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

The black points in each plot represents the theoretical onset overpotential for undoped structures. Top view pictures are inserted in each plot with the corresponding numbering for both the N-doping site and C active sites. Color label of the atoms – C – gray, H - white.

Atom color codes- C – gray, H - white, N - blue, O - red. head.ce:title: Theoretical insights into the activity of the N doped graphene quantum dots for oxygen reduction reaction as a function of the shape and edge terminations

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We have investigated the possible activity of the N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQD) toward the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) using density functional theory (DFT) method. The effect of the shape (triangular (N-GQDT), rhombohedral (N-GQDR) and hexagonal (N-GQDH)), connected both with the zigzag (zz) and armchair (ac) edge terminations of the GQD and with the location of the N atom across the sheets are investigated. The activity of part of the studied N-GQDTac, GQDRac, N-GQDHac and N-GQDHzz structures improves significantly compared to the similar pristine structures. On the other hand, no matter on the location of the N in the N-GQDTzz and in the N-GQDRzz, their activity gets worsen, when compared with the activity of the pristine structures or with the activity of the other four investigated structures. N-GQDTac and N-GQDRac are the structures with the most possible locations of N, that generates active sites. CN bond breaking takes place in the triangular shapes (N-GQDTzz and N-GQDTac) when oxygen adsorption takes place. These bonds are less likely to regenerate and new reaction sites are created. The new reaction site created on the N-GQDTzz represents a possible active site compared to the site generated on the N-GQDTac, that is highly inactive.

The drawing graphical abstract represents the volcano activity plot of oxygen reduction reaction (ORR), using as descriptor the free energy of the first reaction step (the O2 adsorption and protonation on the carbon active sites of the N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs), of various shapes: triangular - T, rhombohedral - R and hexagonal H, and terminations: zigzag (zz) and armchair (ac) – ∆GHOO*-∆GO2) vs. the theoretical onset overpotential (ηonset,th). The most active structures are placed close to the top of the volcano, namely triangular, hexagonal and rhombohedral ones, having armchair terminations, including the hexagonal one, with zigzag terminations. The most active sites are drawn with dark red atoms, closest neighbors to N-doping sites (blue).The reaction mechanism is an inset the red circle, indicating the HOO*/O*/HO* intermediates formation of on the active site (the red filled circle).
Image, graphical abstract

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The overuse of fossil fuels leads to environmental problems that are related to energy production and consumption and requests efforts to achieve energy from renewable sources including solar, rain, waves and wind energy. Fuel cells are still attracting much attention as a clean and sustainable power source. They can directly convert the chemical energy contained in fuels into electric energy with high conversion efficiency, high power density, quiet operation, and less pollution [1,2]. Moreover, the main product of the proton exchange membrane fuel cells (PEM-FC) one of the existing devices on the market, is water, environmentally friendly and fitting the green theme. Generally, the reactions in fuel cells comprise an anodic reaction (hydrogen, methanol, ethanol, or formic acid oxidation etc.) and cathodic reaction (oxygen reduction reaction). The later has the largest portion of electrochemical kinetics loss and system cost, as the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) is a multi-electron process with complicated mechanisms and sluggish kinetics [3,4].

The O2 molecules are reduced by electrons and breaking the O=O bond is electrochemically very difficult, since it possesses an exceptionally strong bond energy of 498 kJ mol−1. The assistance of electrocatalysts for the bond activation and cleavage is necessary to lower the energy barrier. The cathode ORR is more than six orders slower than anode hydrogen oxidation, in aqueous solutions using PEMFCs, and it arises from the varied adsorption/desorption reaction pathways which involve different O-containing intermediates (e.g., OOH*, O*, and OH*) [3]. Presently, by using the best electrocatalysts, Pt-based alloys, the fuel cells have a significant power loss at the cathode side due to a low operation voltage, of 0.7 V as measured with respect to standard hydrogen electrode (SHE), which represents only 57 % of the available free energy [5,6]. Besides this, the high cost, arising from the limited availability, makes the Pt-based alloys electrocatalysts limited to massive applications [7–9] . There are also issues related to stability. Therefore, it is important to develop cost-efficient, highly active and stable ORR electrocatalysts to substitute Pt-based electrodes for large-scale applications of PEMFCs [10–14].

Carbon materials, holds the promise to replace Pt for efficiently ORR catalyzing in fuel cells, owing to their large surface area, good conductivity, tunable morphology, facile preparation, economic viability, fuel tolerance and long-term durability [15,16]. Moreover, carbon materials (especially graphene units) have attracted tremendous interest in the past decade due to the superb electronic arrangement [17] and increased rate of chemical reactions used as electrochromic or catalytic substrates [18]. For more than 10 years, intensive research was done on the N-doped 2D or metal-N dopped planar atom thick carbon nanomaterials of different structures starting with sheets of graphene, nanoribbons, nanotubes and graphene quantum dots [19–28]. Despite of the very intensive work on these materials that exhibits promising ORR catalytic activity, the nature of the active sites and dopants involved are still controversial [29–31] . Moreover, there are still various combinations between dopants and carbon materials that can be further investigated, both from theoretical and experimental point of view. Doping carbon nanomaterials with heteroatoms can effectively tune their intrinsic properties, including electronic characteristics, surface and local chemical features. The N atom, having a comparable atomic size to carbon atoms and five bonding valence electrons, has been widely used for chemical doping of carbon nanomaterials [32–35]. Nitrogen is the most studied dopant in carbon materials because: N atom has a similar atomic radius to C atom, hence it is relatively easy to introduce the controllable amount of N atom into the carbon skeleton; N atom has one free electron more than C atom, which may endow N doped carbon materials with good ORR performance, ORR being electron demand reaction; N atom has a pair of lone pair electrons, which can participate in the large conjugation system of carbon skeleton and increase the conductivity of the carbons. Furthermore, the electronic structure near the N-doped atom has been suggested to drastically change dependence on then atom distance from the edge [36,37]. The improved catalytic performance of N-doped carbon materials comes from the charge density redistribution of adjacent C atoms and is also due to the large electronegativity differences between N and C atoms, when the O2 chemisorption mode is changed and readily facilitates the attraction of electrons from the anode [38].

Developments have been made as well on the zero-dimensional graphene quantum dots (GQDs) associated with quantum-confinement and edge effects [39–44]. The edge-abundant features of GQD are particularly advantageous for electrocatalysts as reactions are more readily electrochemically catalyzed at the edge planes than the basal plane [45–47]. Doping GQDs (Graphene Quantum Dots) correlated with their quantum confinement could produce properties different than those of planar sheets or nanoribbons [32]. Li. et al. have illustrated that nitrogen-functionalized GQDs had highly size-dependent electrocatalytic activity [48].

At theoretical level, M. Dong et al. [49] showed an activity decrease with increasing the size of the dots, for three different sizes of N-GQDs (C23H12N/C53H18N/C95H24N) with hexagonal shapes and zigzag terminations, when N is placed in the second row from the edge. Another similar study carried by J. Nakamura [50] on N-GQDs hexagonally shaped with zigzag terminations of different four sizes (C53H18N/C95H24N/C149H30N/C215H36N) with N placed in the center, showed that the largest structure is the most active. The same authors did test on the C95H24N, when N was substituted for all inequivalent positions and they showed that N placed around the cluster center exhibits higher ORR activity compared to GNC with nitrogen atom in the vicinity of the edge. Beside N, other dopants such as B, S and P were investigated for the same application [45,47,51–54] .Li et collaborators have shown at theoretical level that the N-doped GQD of hexagonal shape and zigzag terminations is the structure that perform better for ORR/OER applications [55]. Owing to their various sizes, shapes, edge terminations, edge functionalization, multiple doping locations on their surfaces – various aspects of their properties are still needed to be investigated.

Therefore, in our work connecting different properties, we use DFT to investigate the activity of various shaped (triangular, rhombohedral, hexagonal) with zigzag and armchair terminations N-doped GQDs in all inequivalent positions towards ORR. To the best of our knowledge, these aspects weren't investigated so far for this class of materials.

The spin polarized DFT calculations were carried out using the atomic simulation environment (ASE) [56] module coupled with GPAW [57–59] ab initio simulation software. The core electrons are described by the projector-augmented wave (PAW) method [60], with the wave function described in a real space grid having the maximum spacing of 0.2 Å. The generalized gradient approximation (GGA) and the Revised-Perdew-Burke-Erzerhof (RPBE) [61] method is used as the exchange-correlation functional. Solvent effects on the surface and adsorbates are included using the implicit solvent model implemented in GPAW as continuum solvent model (CSM) [62] with a dielectric model constant of є = 80, representing water medium, value that is taken from the experiment [63]. The model is based on a smooth cavity g(r) represented on a real space grid. In our previous study, we have shown the differences in the adsorption energies of ORR intermediates without and with the implicit water model and we have shown that the intermediates are stabilized by using this model [46]. Moreover, in the same study we have considered an explicit model as well – that shows even a stronger stabilization of the intermediates – but which do not change significantly the overpotentials of the studied systems compared to the implicit water model. We concluded that applying the implicit solvation method provides a reasonable trend for the ORR across all systems, at a reasonable computational cost. On the other hand, other studies identify its use as critical to correct the energetic description of ORR on N/B dopped graphene [64–66].

The effect of dispersion forces is included by performing perturbative vdW calculations, using BEEF-vdW functional [67]. The atomic positions were fully relaxed until the maximum force on each atom was below 0.05 eV/Å. A vacuum layer of at least 18 Å was included along all axes.

The GQD structures with D3h (triangular shaped - T), D2h (rhombohedral shaped - R), D6h (hexagonal shaped - H) point group symmetries, with the number of carbon atoms slightly varying from one shape to the other (C42 C60), with zigzag (zz) and armchair (ac) edge terminations, were constructed via cutting from a large graphene nanosheet with an ideal structure and CC bond length of 1.42 Å. All models have the edges saturated with hydrogen. We have considered this hydrogen terminated structures, such as to give an understanding of the simplest model possible. Because it is well known that while synthesizing various functional groups (such as O, HO, COOH) could be present on the edge sides, these types of structures could be the subject of investigation in other studies. Eventhough, at experimental level efforts are done to synthesize quantum materials with levels of selectivity and precision that goes beyond the current synthesis tools. Herein we mention the recent work of J. Su and co-workers [68,41] that synthesize nanographene via chemist-intuited atomic robotic probe. On the other hand, we present as the case studies (chapter 3.4) the structures that suffered from CN bond dissociation upon the oxygen adsorption and which are less likely to regenerate, therefore ends in modified structures and other possible active or inactive sites.

The zigzag terminated dots are assigned GQDXzz,CyHz, while the armchair terminated ones are designated GQDXac,CyHz, where X represents the letter of the shape (T - triangle, R - rhombohedral, H - hexagonal), y represents the number of C atoms and z the number of H atoms.

The structures, together with the investigated N-doped sites (numbered from 1 to n) are presented in Fig. 1 as white on a blue background. Depending on the N-location, the C atom, on which the adsorption of ORR moieties is tested, has the same numbering as presented in Fig. 1, with following notation to identify them: Nx-Cy, where x is the N-location and y is C-location on the N-GQD . Some adsorption sites are numbered with ‘ and they represent the equivalent centers, due to the structures symmetry onto adsorption that have been tested.

In general, ORR mainly proceeds via two pathways: two-electron (2e−) pathway, wherein O2 molecules are reduced to hydrogen peroxides (H2O2), and direct four-electron (4e−) pathway, wherein the final reaction product is water (H2O). H2O2 generated by the 2e− reaction, might corrode a carbon-based electrocatalyst, causing poor device durability. Thus, 4e− pathway is more desirable than 2e− pathway; hence, the selectivity for 4e− pathway is required for carbon-based catalyst.

To predict the activity of these systems, we assume there is an O2 reduction to H2O through the intermediates HOO*, O*, HO* (HOO* formation -> HOO* dehydration -> O* -> HO* formation -> HOH formation), an associative mechanism that imply the 4e− pathway, in the acidic environment:
(1) O2 + * + 4H+ + 4e−+* -> HOO* + 3H+ + 3e− ∆G1
(2) HOO* + 3H+ + 3e− -> O* + H2O +2H++ 2e− ∆G2
(3) H2O + O* + 2H++ 2e− -> HO* + H2O + H + + e − ∆G3
(4) H2O + HO* + H + + e −-> * + 2H2O ∆G4
where “*” denotes free catalyst sites and the ORR intermediates are adsorbed onto respective catalyst site. The electrocatalytic activities are dominated by the stability of intermediates adsorption. The ∆G1-∆G4 are the free energies of the intermediate steps, and are based on the computational hydrogen electrode (CHE) [64] model, helping to identify the ORR thermodynamic determining step, since the chemical potential (the free energy per H) for the reaction (H + + e −) is equal to 1/2H2, by setting the reference potential to the standard hydrogen electrode under standard conditions (pH = 0, P(H2) = 1 bar, and T = 298 K).

According to this model, the reaction free energy (∆G) is redefined and is given by the expression:
(5) ∆G1–4 = ∆G1–4 + ∆GU + ∆GpH + ∆Gfield
where ∆GU is defined as -neU, where U is the potential at the electrode relative to the standard hydrogen electrode, n is the number of the transferred electrons in each consecutive step and e is the charge of an electron. Additionally, ∆GpH is expressed as kBTln10(pH), kB Boltzmann constant, T = 298.15 K and ∆Gfield is the effect of the local electric field near the surface of the electrode, not taken into account, since the absolute value is negligible small (≈10−2 eV) [69]. For ORR calculations in acidic electrolyte, we consider pH = 0. The free energy correction due to the electrochemical double layer is small so that neglected as in previous studies [64]. Therefore, at pH = 0 and U = 0, the expression of the free energy for each step is:
(6) ∆G1 = ∆GHOO* - ∆GO2
(7) ∆G2 = ∆GO* - ∆GHOO*
(8) ∆G3 = ∆GHO* - ∆GO*
(9) ∆G4 = ∆GH2O - ∆GHO*
where ∆GO2/∆GHOO*/∆GO*/∆GHO*/∆GH2O represent free energies of the O2/HOO*/O*/HO* intermediates and water. O2 molecule cannot be accurately described in DFT computations, owing to the high-spin ground state. Therefore, the free energy of O2 is heuristically estimated by setting the water dissociation equilibrium potential at 1.23 V, under standard conditions. Note - ∆GORR is the ORR overall formation free energy (2H2 + O2 → 2H2O) set at 25 °C, p(H2) = p(O2) = 1 bar (∆GORR = −4.92 eV) experimental value and it is used for calculating the O2 free energy. Consequently, all free energies of the intermediate steps are calculated relative to H2O(l) and H2(g). ∆GH2O is zero, water and proton electron pairs being used as free molecule references for the adsorption energies. The free energy of adsorption for the adsorbed moieties is expressed as:
(10) ∆Gads = ∆Eads+∆ZPE-T∆S
where ∆Eads is the DFT adsorption energy, ∆ZPE and ∆S being differences in zero-point energies and entropies, respectively. T is the room temperature, T = 298.15 K, all over this paper. The zero-point energies and entropy calculated values are summarized in Table S1.

The calculated adsorption energies of all three intermediates (ΔEHOO* s/ΔEO* s/ΔEHO* s) using the implicit solvent model (s) are done relative to H2O and H2 in vacuum (EH2O v, EH2 v), by subtracting the energies of the isolated adsorbate and the surface catalyst from the total energy of the adsorbed system, as follows:
(11) ΔEHOO* s = EHOO* s - E* s - (2EH2O v - 1.5EH2 v)
(12) ∆EHO* = EHO* s – E* s – (EH2O v – 0.5EH2 v)
(13) ∆EO* = EO* s – E* s – (EH2O v – EH2 v)
The remaining terms EHOO* s/EO* s/EHO* s/E* s are DFT ground state energies of the surfaces with and without adsorbates. The adsorption is considered on top of the carbon sites.
The 4e− pathway ORR is ideally facilitated just below the standard equilibrium potential (Uo = 1.23 V).

The ORR activity of the catalyst can be evaluated by the theoretical onset overpotential (ηonset,th) and thermodynamic limiting potential (Ulim), where all reaction steps are exothermic. The limiting potential (Ulim) can be calculated by determining the highest free energy increase of all reaction steps.
(14) Ulim = −max {∆G1, ∆G2, ∆G3, ∆G4}/e −

The theoretical onset overpotential (ηonset,th) can be calculated from Eq. (15). A lower overpotential implies a better ORR activity.
(15) ηonset,th = 1.23 − Ulim = 1.23 + max{∆G1, ∆G2, ∆G3, ∆G4}/e −
The overpotential was calculated for all studied adsorption sites.

We have studied by using DFT, from the thermodynamic point of view, how the doping with N modifies the activity for ORR of the GQD with various shapes and edge terminations compared to the pristine structures.

Fig. 2 shows the relative formation energies of the N-GQDs. The N atom is preferentially located at the edges, rather than on inner graphene dots, no matter on the shape and the edge terminations. This agrees well with many previous results for various other types of N-doped graphene sheets [24,70–72]. For the N-GQDRzz, the N atom is preferentially located on site numbered 2, which is the neighbor site to vertex location (60° angle). For the N-GQDRac, the N atom has the preferentially location at site numbered 9, closest neighbor to the vertex of larger angle (120°). For the other shapes, the preferred location is in the middle of the edge sites, as follows: for N-GQDTzz the preferred site is located at position 6, for N-GQDHzz is located at site 3, for N-GQDTac is located at site 6 and for N-GQDHac is located at site 1. In the studied structures, N is protonated, that most likely happens within an acidic environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Trends in the adsorption energies. correlations with the electronic states
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorption properties of the ORR intermediates on the N-GQDs are calculated according to Eqs. (10)–(13) for all inequivalent carbon atoms considered closest neighbors to each N. In the previous reports that comprise N doped nanocarbons [73,30,74], these carbon atoms sites have been shown to be the active ones towards ORR .

The plot containing the relationships between ∆GHOO*/∆GO* and ∆GHO* is displayed in Fig. 3 . The corresponding relationships can be well fitted based on following eqs.:
(16) ∆GHOO* = ∆GHO* + 3.11 eV
(17) ∆GO* = 0.66 ∆GHO* + 1.08 eV

As shown in Fig. 3, the data points that represents the relation between ∆GHOO* and ∆GHO* (Eq. (16)) have the slope and the intercept with R2 > 0.98, similar to the other scaling relationships for other materials (oxides/metals etc.) [75,76]. For the last ≈15 years, the Eq. (16) has been shown to slightly vary from the first predicted one (∆GHOO* = ∆GHO* + 3.2 eV) [77,78] that was established for the oxide materials to ∆GHOO* = ∆GHO* + 3.1/3.05 eV [79]. On the other hand, the mentioned variations come from the materials type, data quantity, the used functionals etc. It is well known that many materials suitable for ORR are strongly dependent on the relation given in Eq. (16) [80,75] . The relation between ∆GO* and ∆GHO* (Eq. (17)) exhibits a much poorer link to R2 (0.53 instead of 0.98). This is due to the adsorption of several oxygen atoms in the bridging position, or to CN bond breaks. Therefore, within the scaling relation there are oxygen atoms with different bonding type.

Further analysis on the trends of the adsorption energies show there is at least one position of N for each shape that enhances significantly the adsorption energies of the ORR moieties. The strongest adsorption energies for HO* are in the range 0.1 – 0.4 eV, while the weakest are in the range 1.4 - 1.8 eV. Therefore, by doping on each inequivalent site, a wide range of adsorption energies were obtained which reflects as well in the wide range of possible activities.

On the GQDTzz and GQDRzz, the N-doping sites that induce the strongest adsorption energies, are located along the second rows counted from the structure edge and induce the stronger adsorption to neighboring carbon atoms placed on the edge (ex. N3-C4 sites on GQDTzz – see Fig. S1a, N7-C6 on GQDRzz etc. – see Fig. S1c). When N-doping is located on the other sites (right on the edges or on the basal planes), the closest neighboring carbon sites have the weakest adsorption out of all six studied structures. For the remaining four structures to be analyzed, in the case of the N-GQDTac (purple dots in Fig. S1b),N-GQDRac (orange dots in Fig. S1d), N-GQDHzz (cyan dots in Fig. S1e) and N-GQDHac (pink dots in Fig. S1f), when N is placed right on the edge, together with their closest neighboring C atom, also placed on the edge, the moieties are the strongest adsorbed in most structures. Moreover, the adsorption energies increase as the N-doping sites are located towards to the middle of the structures.

The O2 electroreduction can produce either H2O2 via 2e− pathway or H2O through 4e− pathway. After the protonation of O2 and formation of HOO* moiety (O2+H ++e − ->HOO*), the further protonation of HOO* can proceed to forming H2O2 (HOO*+H ++e − -> H2O2(l)) or H2O(l) (HOO*+H ++e − - > O* + H2O(l). Since the surfaces may follow one of the above mentioned two mechanisms, the potential free energy for H2O2 formation should be smaller/or higher than that of the HOO* protonation to O* and H2O. Consequently, the Gibbs free energy of O* (∆GO*) on the carbon active sites for a high selectivity towards H2O, should be less positive than 3.52 eV (∆GH2O2 - ∆GH2O) [81]. Our results show that the ∆GO* for all investigated N-GQDs is less than 3.52 eV (see the position of the points for ∆GO* and the dashed red line in Fig. 3), which indicates selectivity for water formation. In this case we investigate the ORR activity using the associative 4e− mechanism.

To understand part of the trends, we discuss herein aspects related to the electronic properties, namely the band gap of each carbon site that could correlate with their adsorption energies and accordingly with their ORR activities.

For the description of the p-density of states of the carbon atoms, the same GGA functionals are used. Despite the fact that it is well known that these functionals are not ideal for the band gap approximations, it does provide reliable adsorption energies, which are the main parameters used herein for description of the activities [82,83,67]. Therefore, we will use the p states as a general guidance in understanding in a certain measure the trends in the adsorption energies.

We have showed in previous work for the edge of the graphene nanocarbons, that there is a connection between the adsorption strength and the corresponding band gap. As the band gap decreases the adsorption energy get stronger. Definitely by N-doping, the electronic structure of the neighboring carbon atoms is modified. These changes are pictured in Figs. 4 and S2–S7, where we show the differences in PDOS (projected density of states) for each carbon closest neighbor to N in N-doped structures (solid lines), and the same carbon atoms from the undoped structures (dashed lines). Various changes take place: transitions from the semiconducting to the conducting character, transition from small gaps semiconductors to semimetal character, a reducing of the band gap or change of the positions of the conduction and valence bands.

In our investigated structures, despite doping, there are sites for which the band gap remains significant. The most sites with significant band gap remaining after doping are located on N-GQDTzz and on N-GQDRzz. For N-GQDTzz, these sites are: N1-C2, N2-C1, N2-C3, N4-C3, N4-C5, N6-C5, N7-C3, N9-C5, N9-C8, N9-C10 (see PDOS in Fig. S2), while for N-GQDRzz the sites are: N3-C14, N4-C3, N5-C12, N6-C5, N8-C7, N9-C7, N9-C10, N12-C5, N14-C3, N14-C13 (see PDOS states in Fig. S3). For the other four remaining structures, there are fewer carbon sites with band gap . On N-GQDHzz, there are N1-C2, N2-C1, N4-C6, N6-C4 (see PDOS in Fig. S4), on N-GQDTac there are N3-C4, N4-C3, N7-C11, N8-C10, N9-C9’, N10-C12 (see PDOS in Fig. S5), on N-GQDRac N3-C4, N4-C3, N5-C4, N7-C8, N11-C11 (see PDOS in Fig. S6), while on N-GQDHac -N5-C4 (see PDOS in Fig. S7). On most of these sites, the HOO* moiety is unstable (leaving the surface during optimization), while with few exceptions on rest of them, the adsorption energies are the weakest (higher than 4.2 - 4.5 eV). For the structures where the HOO* moiety was unstable, we haven't performed further calculations for the O* and HO* moieties.

For the other sites there is a significant change of the band gaps, going from a semiconducting character to a conducting-like character. In general, these transitions to metallic character induce stronger adsorption energies for all moieties. In Fig. 4, it is presented the band gap changes of the sites that adsorb the strongest the ORR intermediates for each N-GQD. On the C4 site next to the N3 of the N-GQDTzz shape, there is a change from a narrow band gap semiconductor, existing within the undoped structure, to a semimetal character in the N-doped one (Fig. 4a– red dashed (undoped) to solid (N-doped) line). For N-GQDTac, there is a transition from the mid band gap semiconductor to the metallic character, for both N1-C1’ and N1-C2 sites (Fig. 4b- green and red dashed (undoped) and solid lines(N-doped)). Similar changes are seen for the carbon sites of the other four structures N7-C6 in N-GQDRzz (red dashed and solid lines in Fig. 4c), N8-C9 in N-GQDRac (green dashed and red solid lines in Fig. 4d), N1-C1’in N-GQDHzz (red dashed and solid lines in Fig. 4e), N2-C2 in N-GQDHac (red dashed (undoped) and solid (N-doped) in Fig. 4f).

Beside these sites, we enumerate other ones that adsorb slightly weaker the ORR intermediates than the strongest ones (adsorption energies for HOO* bellow 4.15 eV or corresponding adsorption energies for HO* below 1.2 eV) and suffering transitions from the large band gaps to metallic character. This metallic characters is given by the corresponding p-states of the respective carbon atoms: on N-GQDRzz the part of the sites are: N1-C2,N3-C2, N5-C4,N6-C7,N7-C6 etc. (Fig. S3), on N-GQDHzz the sites: N2-C3, N4-C5, N5-C4, N6-C6, N5-C2, N5-C8, N2-C5 (Fig. S4), on N-GQDTac the sites: N2-C1, N2-C3, N3-C2, N5-C6, N6-C5, N8-C7, N7-C6 etc. (see in Fig. S5), on N-GQDRac part of the sites are: N10-C3’, N2-C1, N6-C5, N5-C6, N3-C2, N1-C2, N15-C4, N4-C5 etc. (Fig. S6) and for GQDHac N3-C1, N1-C2, N5-C5’, N4-C3, N3-C3’(Fig. S7).

Trends concerning the decrease/strengthening of the adsorption energies with the decrease of the band gap were shown in other previous studies as well [52,55]. Clearly these changes in adsorption energies reflects directly in the final predicted activities.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Prediction of the ORR catalytic performance
ce:section.ce:para: In the below figure (Fig. 5 ), the trends are illustrated for each N dopped structure for the closest neighboring carbon sites that gives the best theoretical onset overpotential (ηonset,th), considering only N inequivalent location within the six shapes. The activity is represented by plotting ηonset,th against free energy of the first intermediate reaction step (∆G1). A volcano shaped plot is obtained, with best performing sites placed closer to the volcano top, with values varying between 0.3 and 1.6 V. In each plot we have added the best performing edge site of the undoped GQD structures.

The dashed line volcano represents the general ORR trends, using the scaling relations between ∆GHOO* = ∆GHO* + 3.11 eV. For the structures placed on the right side of the volcano, the potential limiting step (PLS) is the HOO* formation, where the adsorption is weak (the weak side of volcano and PLS is generally determined by the adsorbing steps). For the points placed on the left side of the volcano plot, the formation of the second water molecule (ΔG4) is the PLS. On the respective sites the intermediates adsorb stronger when shifting towards its left (the strong side of the volcano and PLS are mainly determined by desorbing steps). The points that are not placed on the volcano have either the PLS one of other two steps (∆G2, ∆G3), or have suffered from structural changes during the reaction (see Fig. 4a brown points designating N1-C2 and N3-C2 structures). Some of these points will be discussed separately in the study case chapter.

According to this trend, in the case of the N-GQDTzz and N-GQDRzz shapes, the best performing sites are the one belonging to the pristine structures that are placed on the edge (C2 - Fig. 5a and C4 - Fig. 5c). For the others, the best performing sites are the closest neighbor carbon atoms to N atoms. Therefore, for the N-GQDTac the most active site is C8 neighbor to the 7th N (Fig. 5b), for the N-GQDRac it is C15 neighbor to 14th N (Fig. 5d), for the N-GQDHzz it is C6’th neighbor to 6th N (Fig. 5e) and for N-GQDHac C3’ neighbor to the 3rd N (Fig. 5f).

Consequently, in the case of the N-GQDTzz (Fig. 5a) and N-GQDRzz (Fig. 5c), the activity is not improved by GQDs N-doping, compared to the active sites of the undoped GQDs edges (see Fig. 5a and c). On the other hand, the armchair terminated triangular (Fig. 5b) and rhombohedral (Fig. 5d) shapes have a significantly improvement activities towards ORR by N-doping. The last-mentioned shapes have many possible active sites placed close to the volcano top . This approach represents a good choice of materials since the activity for this structure is less sensible to N-location. N-doped graphene could be easier experimentally synthesized without the condition of being in locations fitted for desired activity, especially that there are some predicted N-GQDTac active structures with N placed on the edge. These structures with N atoms on the edge are the most stable ones, so that the easiest to be synthesized.

On N-GQDTac (Fig. 5b) the most active sites are located to the edge vicinity . N is located either on the second row from the edge, or directly on the edge, while the carbon active sites are located inside the sheet (see N7-C8/N5-C4/N6-C7). For N-GQDRac, some doping sites are found closer to the sheet center (N13-C13’, N14-C15 - Fig. 5d), or closer to the edge, as in the case of N-GQDTac (N7-C14, N5-C14-Fig. 5d).

On the hexagonal shapes, one may observe two trends: for the N-GQDHzz (Fig. 5e) most sites are placed on weak binding side of the volcano, while for the N-GQDHac (Fig. 5f) most sites are placed on the strong binding side. In both cases, the active sites belong to inner part of the basal planes of the sheets. On the N-GQDHac, the site with the highest predicted activity is closer to the dot center (N6-C6’ - Fig. 5f). J. Nakamura et al. reported similar findings with the N atom located around the center of the cluster on a hexagonal zigzag shaped structure [24,50]. Another possible active site, with similar activity to N6-C6’, is placed on the basal plane, as well, being slightly closer to the edge vicinity (N4-C5 - Fig. 5f). However, on the N-GQDHac, the most possible active site is located closer to the edge and not to the sheet center (N3’ – C3 in Fig. 5e).

Placing the most active sites inside GQDs represents an issue from the experimental point of view, because N atoms preference for the edge makes difficult to synthesize N-GQDs, containing N atoms inside the structure, to be in thermal equilibrium. However, there are attempts using nonequilibrium processes such as solution plasma [84,85] or other processes [86] to obtain N-GQDs with N atoms in the basal plane

In the presence of N, the carbon edges start to interact too strong with the moieties, hence, they are placed on strong side of the volcano plot, towards high onset overpotentials (i.e. N3-C4 in N-GQDTzz - Fig. 5a/N1-C2 in N-GQDTac - Fig. 5b/ N8-C8’ in N-GQDRzz – Fig. 5c/N10-C9 in N-GQDRac - Fig. 5d/N1-C1’ in N-GQDHzz -Fig. 5e/N2-C1 in GQDHac – Fig. 5f).

In the attempt to relate the volcano plot activity to the corresponding PDOS, all sites from the left side, from around the top and a part of points from the right side of the volcano (with ηonset,th up to ≈ 1 V) have a metallic character, while the rest of the sites from the right side that have the ηonset,th >1 V, have a semiconducting characters. Therefore, as shown in our previous study the activity of GQD's is strongly connected to the size of the bandgap. For the undoped structures we have shown that the highest activity is for the sites that have either a narrow band gap or have a conductive character, while the activity decreases with increasing the band gap [46]. In this study all sites with the activity close to the top of the volcano have a conductive character. On the right side of the volcano the activity decreases with increasing the bandgap.

Because herein we considered shapes that have approximately the same size, we discuss some possible trends for other sizes. These trends are derived by combining the information from this study and from the previous one [46], where the activity of the edges of differently sized nanoclusters was predicted. We foresee that by N doping the GQDTzz and GQDRzz structures of different sizes, their behaviors to be similar with the ones presented here and their activities to be lower than of the similar undoped structures. This behavior is mostly because by N doping induces larger band gaps of the C sites. For the armchair terminated ones and for the GQDHzz no matter on the size by N doping, we foreseen for each shape points residing close to the top of the volcano. Moreover, the trends in the adsorption energies will surely obey the scaling relations between HOO* and HO*: ∆GHOO*=∆GHO*+3.11 eV which indicates a minimum overpotential of 0.3 eV for most of the active sites. This improvement of the activity going from the undoped to the doped one, is possible due to the fact that by doping part of the N next neighboring sites will become conductive as it was shown in the present study, especially that the bandgap of the undoped structures decreases with increasing the size. The CN bond dissociation upon oxygen adsorption is possible to take place in the triangular shapes in the similar positions.
Overall, the shape and the edge terminations, correlated with the N-doping sites, create a variety of activities.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Case studies – structural changes during ORR reaction – new possible active sites or inactivation of the active ones
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the high reactivity of the edges, structural modifications are prone to take place. In these studies, we have encountered CN bond breaking, where O is attached to some of the neighboring carbon sites. Similar CN bond dissociations with ring opening in graphene like structures were previously shown to take place [21,74].

The reaction centers, where these changes take place, are within N-GQDTzz structure at N1-C2 and N3-C2 sites. Once these bonds are broken, it might be less likely that part of structures would be or not regenerated differently than the initial ones.

We have performed supplementary calculations on the sites placed in the proximity of the CN bond modifications. The free energy diagrams of these structures are plotted in Fig. 6 . Therefore, when the reaction is tested to take place on the N1-C2 structure, with C2 being the active site, at O adsorption the CN bond breaks (see the green solid line in Fig. 6a and the corresponding inset top view structures in line with the green N1-C2). When the O is protonated, CN bond is regenerated. This regeneration is an endothermic step at U = 1.23 V that necessitates an energy of approximately 0.84 eV (indicated by the thick dark green line in Fig. 6a) and represents the PLS. On the modified broken structure, we have tested the possibility of using as the reaction site the C2’ atom next to the N1-C2(O) configuration (with O co-adsorbate on C2, see the inset top view structures in line with the red N1-C2(O)-C2’ in Fig. 6a). The free energy diagram is marked with the solid red line in Fig. 6a. The new site built by our test seems to be active towards ORR. The HO* protonation and release is PLS with a 0.3 eV free energy (dark red solid line in Fig. 6a). The HO* moiety is stabilized on the surface by the aid of the hydrogen intramolecular bond, that is linked to O from the C2 site. Along the reaction path, HOO* is stabilized by the same hydrogen bond, as well. In the same structure, when the ORR intermediates are adsorbed on the C2’ site, CN bond is regenerated. It seems that N-doping, followed by the structural changes after O adsorption, has the capacity to lead to active sites compared to the initial structure. According to the theoretical onset overpotential, the new active site is placed to the volcano top, with a relatively small predicted value (brown point of the N1-C2(O)-C2 structure in Fig. 5a with ηonset,th = 0.3 V). After the adsorption of O on C2 site, similar behavior appears in the vicinity of the N3-C2 dissociated bond. This ends in the N3-C2(O) structure with the tested C4 as the reaction site and that is next to the pyridinic N (see the inset top view pictures in line with the blue N3-C2(O)-C4. For this site, also, PLS is the O2 adsorption and protonation (blue solid line in Fig. 6a). PLS is placed close to the volcano top plot, but it has a slightly higher predicted onset overpotential (brown point corresponding to N3-C2(O)-C4 in Fig. 5a).

Another bond breaking that might take place is in N-GQDTac, where oxygen intermediate is formed on C7’ site, i.e. next to N7 (see the inset top view pictures in line with the blue N7-C7’ in Fig. 6a). It is less likely to have an initial adsorption of HOO* on this site, since the other two carbon sites (C8 and C6) adsorb it stronger. But, depending on the ORR conditions, this adsorbate could be present on this C7’ carbon site. When the reaction takes place on C8, the theoretical onset potential is among the smallest, placed ontop of the volcano plot (Figs. 5b and 6b– the red solid line for the N7-C8 structure and the inset top view picture in line with the red N7-C8).If the reaction takes place on N7-C7’, the PLS is the formation of the HOO* moiety (blue solid line on Fig. 6b, PLS being shown the thick dark blue line) and is estimated of having a theoretical onset overpotential of ηth,onset= 0.71 eV. As in the case of the N-GQDTzz, the dissociated CN could remain stable under this form. New possible formed sites have been tested, namely the C6 site in the dissociated structure – N7-C7’(O)-C6 structure. Unfortunately, this C6 site, next to the pyridinic N, doesn't show any activity, due to the high theoretical onset overpotential determined by the formation of the HOO* moiety (ηonset,th = 1.6 V, green line in Fig. 6b and PLS shown by the thick dark green line).
Therefore, while in the case of the N-GQDTzz, the active sites are obtained by the CN dissociation, for N-GQDTac the activity decreases considerably.

The ORR activity of the triangular (T), rhombohedral (R) and hexagonal (H) GQDs, with armchair (ac) and zigzag (zz) terminations, N-doped in all inequivalent positions were investigated using DFT. For all six investigated structures, the most active sites are placed inside the sheets, but closer to the edges than to the basal plane. The structures that have the highest number of N locations, inducing high activities (low theoretical onset overpotentials ηonset,th), are the N-GQDTac and N-GQRac. For all cases, the doping positions are in the proximity of the edges, but not on the edges. In the case of the N-GQDTzz and N-GQDRzz, the N-doping doesn't induce any activity, and the most active site remains the predicted edges of the pristine structures. The increased activities of the N doped structures are registered as well on both hexagonal shapes with armchair and zigzag terminations. For these two structures, the flexibility, concerning the location of N across the sheet, is limited and there are only two such places of N on each structure that give activities close to the top of the volcano. On some of the N-GQDTzz and N-GQDTac structures, CN bond breaking takes place at O adsorption. The dissociated bond creates other possible active sites. On N-GQDTzz, a new active site is made, while in the case of N-GQDTac, no any predicted activity can be foreseen for the new site. Correlations are established between the trends of the adsorption energies of the ORR intermediates (HOO*/O*/HO*) and the band gap of each carbon site. The sites that adsorb the weakest present a band gap, while the others ones that start to adsorb stronger and stronger have a semimetallic or metallic character. The structures with the highest number of carbon sites, that have band gaps and corresponding weak adsorption energies, are the zigzag terminated triangles and rhombohedral shapes.
